title-group.article-title: Penalized regression with negative-unlabeled data: an approach to developing a Long COVID research index

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/002pd6e78grid.32224.350000 0004 0386 9924Biostatistics, Massachusetts General Hospital, Boston, MA USA
2https://ror.org/03vek6s52grid.38142.3c000000041936754XDepartment of Medicine, Harvard Medical School, Boston, MA, USA

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Moderate to severe Long COVID is estimated to impact as many as 10% of SARS-CoV-2 infected individuals, representing a chronic condition with a substantial public health burden. An expansive literature has identified over 200 persistent symptoms associated with a history of SARS-CoV-2 infection; yet, there remains to be a clear consensus on a syndrome definition. Long COVID thus represents a “negative-unlabeled” outcome where those without prior infection must be Long COVID “negative” but those with prior infection have unknown or “unlabeled” Long COVID status. Despite this lack of a gold standard definition or biomarker, developing and evaluating an approach to characterizing Long COVID is a critical first step in future studies of risk and resiliency factors, mechanisms of disease, and interventions for both treatment and prevention.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We recently applied a strategy for defining a numeric Long COVID research index (LCRI) using Lasso-penalized logistic regression, leveraging information on history of SARS-CoV-2 infection as a pseudo-label. In the current manuscript we formalize and evaluate this approach in a simulation framework for the occurrence of infection, Long COVID onset, and symptomatology. We evaluate its performance selecting symptoms associated with Long COVID and distinguishing individuals with Long COVID, in the presence of symptom correlations and demographic confounders. We compare the LCRI method to a simpler index defined by counting Long COVID symptoms, and assess these methods in a reanalysis of data on participants enrolled in the Adult Cohort of the Researching COVID to Enhance Recovery (RECOVER) study.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Simulation results demonstrate that the Lasso-penalized LCRI methodology appropriately selects symptoms associated with Long COVID, and that the LCRI has high discriminatory power to distinguish Long COVID, outperforming symptom count. This performance was robust to correlation between symptoms, and weighting methods are shown to successfully address potential confounding by demographic characteristics. Analysis of RECOVER data showed the LCRI outperforming symptom count by misclassifying fewer uninfected individuals as having Long COVID.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: As the LCRI is increasingly used to characterize LC in research settings, this paper represents an important step in understanding its operating characteristics and developing general methodology for settings with negative-unlabeled data.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12874-025-02737-5.

institution-wrap.institution: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2026

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Globally, over 778 million cases of COVID-19 have been reported as of October 1, 2025, resulting in over 7 million deaths [1]. Among those who survive the acute phase of SARS-CoV-2 infection, many continue to experience long-term and often debilitating symptoms for months or even years, which collectively are known as Long COVID (LC) and sometimes referred to as post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 (PASC). Estimates of LC incidence vary widely across studies, ranging from 5 to 25% [2], due to differences in study design as well as inconsistent LC classifiers [3].

In the absence of a gold-standard definition, we recently developed methodology to define a symptom-based LC research index (LCRI) with versions for adults, and children ages 0–5, 6–11, and 12–17, based on an informative subset of features [4–7]. This approach uses a Lasso-penalized logistic regression model, with history of SARS-CoV-2 infection as the outcome to serve as a partial label for LC status, and presence of long-term symptoms as features [8]. The fitted model is expected to identify symptoms associated with LC, and uses the estimated coefficients of those symptoms to define a quantitative index correlating with the likelihood of having LC. The LCRI for adults has been used in numerous studies of LC, including to evaluate LC in pregnant individuals [9], clinical laboratory abnormalities [10], and sex differences [11].

Using infection as a pseudo-label for LC reflects the fact that individuals with a history of SARS-CoV-2 infection may or may not have LC, while individuals without a history of SARS-CoV-2 infection cannot have LC. As a result, infection history and LC status form a partially-observed outcome referred to as “negative-unlabeled” data: those without history of infection are necessarily LC “negative”, while those with history of infection are “unlabeled” [12]. This is a variant of the more common “positive-unlabeled” data sometimes arising from electronic health records, in which some health characteristics are documented (“positive”) but lack of documentation does not mean the characteristic is absent (“unlabeled”) [13]. Existing ‘semi-supervised’ methods for partially-labeled data commonly leverage subsets of individuals with definitive positive and negative documentation [14], however in the absence of a gold-standard definition no individuals have a “positive” observed LC status. Other techniques include two-step algorithms in which labels are inferred for a ‘reliable’ subset of the unlabeled group, and then combined with the labeled data to develop a classifier [15]. Instead, the use of infection status as a pseudo-label leverages the fact that the population of infected individuals is a mixture of those with and without LC, and therefore symptoms associated with LC will also be more common among infected individuals, while symptoms not associated with LC are expected to be indistinguishable between infected and uninfected individuals [16].

Nevertheless, it remains unclear whether an analysis strategy based on infection status as a pseudo-label can appropriately capture information about latent Long COVID status. Moreover, LC symptoms exhibit correlation which may affect the selection performance of Lasso-penalized logistic regression, and therefore the resulting LCRI. Additionally, in practice LC studies enroll infected and uninfected populations that may differ with respect to baseline covariates that are potentially associated with the presence of long-term symptoms—the resulting potential for confounding, and impact of techniques to mitigate confounding, remains unclear.

To address these gaps, we investigate operating characteristics of the LCRI methodology using a rigorous conceptual framework for LC onset and symptoms. We evaluate whether this approach correctly identifies a subset of symptoms that associate with LC, and whether the LCRI has high discriminatory power for detecting LC. We further explore balancing weights as a method for handling differential sampling by baseline demographics. Finally, we evaluate the relative performance of this approach compared to a simpler scoring framework defined by counting prevalent long-term symptoms. Evaluation of symptom count as an alternative to the LCRI reflects existing efforts to define LC as inclusive of broad symptomatology after SARS-CoV-2 infection, such as recent emphasis by the National Academy of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine that LC can manifest as any of hundreds of symptoms affecting every organ of the body [17].

There remains a pressing need for a standardized research definition that rigorously identifies patients with LC while maintaining a high degree of specificity [18]. As such, arriving at a data-driven and discriminatory research index is a significant first step to further research into the risk and resiliency factors as well as the biological mechanisms of LC onset and recovery.

sec.title: Conceptual framing and data generating mechanism
LCRI methodology
sec.p: This methodology reflects the hypotheses that the features selected in fitting the model of Eq. (2) will be associated with LC, and that the resulting LCRI of Eq. (3) will have strong discriminatory power for differentiating individuals with and without LC, as higher LCRI values will be correlated with higher likelihood of having LC.

sec.title: Balancing weights to address potential confounding
Simulation studies
sec.p: In addition to these primary simulations, we also performed additional simulations assessing three phenomena: 1) the impact of balancing weighting on performance under potential confounding; 2) specification of the elastic net penalty as an alternative to the Lasso [19], which is known to be more likely to select groups of correlated covariates, whereas the Lasso tends to select one covariate from amongst a set of correlated covariates [22]; and 3) the presence of infection misclassification in which individuals who truly have a history of infection may be ‘observed’ to have no history of infection (e.g., people who had an asymptomatic infection that went undetected), or that individuals who truly do not have a history of infection may be ‘observed’ to have history of infection (e.g., individuals who did not actually have a SARS-CoV-2 infection, but believed they did due to another illness with similar acute symptoms as COVID-19). These simulations are detailed in Appendix A of the supplementary materials, with results summarized below.

sec.title: Data Application to RECOVER-Adult
sec.p: A total of 37 candidate symptoms from 12 clinically-defined categories were considered in the cross-sectional analysis, including cardiac, gastrointestinal, neurologic, musculoskeletal, and others. The full symptom list, with symptom rates by infection status, is available in Appendix B of the supplementary materials.

Using the balancing-weighted results we compute an LCRI value for every individual, and graphically compare the distribution to the distribution of symptom counts in this cohort. To compare the performance of the LCRI and symptom count to distinguish individuals with and without LC, we consider their discriminative value if a score threshold were used to classify LC status. Because the true LC status of each individual in the dataset is unavailable, we consider the proportion of infected and uninfected individuals who would be classified as having LC under varying thresholds. These results are presented using a cross-validated receiver operator characteristic (ROC) curve, in which we fit the Lasso-penalized logistic regression model in each possible subset of 90% of the data and estimate the index and resulting curve in the remaining 10%, and average across the results. Improved discriminative performance corresponds to reducing the proportion of uninfected classified as having LC for a given proportion of infected classified as having LC.

sec.title: Simulation results
sec.p: Importantly, the Lasso-penalized model tended to be conservative in variable selection, with generally high true negative rates in the uncorrelated and group sparse settings, but modest true positive rates that also varied depending on symptom correlation. We also observed that the estimated coefficients were relatively highly rank-correlated with the true LC associations. However, under non-group sparse correlated symptoms the rank-correlation between estimated and true associations weakened, as symptoms with no true effect were more likely to have a nonzero coefficient estimate.

As shown in Fig. 1, the LCRI derived from Lasso-penalized logistic regression on infection status showed reasonable capacity to discriminate between those with and without LC across all settings, with improved performance as signal strength increases.

The impact of symptom correlation on LCRI discrimination was modest for the regression-based LCRI, with slightly better performance in settings under which larger models tended to be selected (i.e., non-group sparsity), and slightly worse performance when smaller models tended to be selected (i.e., group sparsity). This seems to indicate that LCRI performance does not substantially degrade even when only some true non-zero symptoms are selected into the index, particularly under group sparsity in which LC associated symptoms have strong correlation.

Across all settings, the symptom count consistently underperformed relative to the regression-based LCRI. In particular, the symptom count notably degraded in performance in the presence of correlation between symptoms.

Finally, we summarize the results of additional simulation studies presented in Appendix A of the supplementary materials, to address a range of additional considerations. In simulations evaluating the discriminative performance of the LCRI in the presence of a confounding covariate positively associated both with the probability of infection and the probability of having symptoms, using balancing weights as described in the “Balancing weights to address potential confounding” section yielded improved performance in settings where confounding may otherwise obscure the true associations, relative to an unweighted analysis. In simulations comparing the LCRI methodology using the Lasso penalty to use of the elastic net penalty, there was almost no difference between Lasso penalization and elastic net penalization, perhaps due to the large sample size relative to covariate dimension. However, differences may be more pronounced in other settings, and could be examined in future research. A final simulation study evaluated scenarios with increasing rates of infection misclassification under the medium signal strength, and found that both selection performance and LCRI discriminative performance showed only minor degradation. In the most extreme setting of high misclassification and no symptom correlation, LCRI discriminative performance approached that of the symptom count, but continued to perform better in general.

sec.title: Application to RECOVER-Adult
sec.p:
From the 37 candidate symptoms shown in Appendix B, Table B.2 with frequencies by infection status, Fig. 2 shows the symptoms selected using the Lasso-penalized logistic regression, with and without balancing weights. In the weighted model a total of 13 symptoms were selected, and the symptoms with the largest coefficients—and therefore largest contributions to the index—were loss or change of smell/taste, post-exertional malaise (PEM), chronic cough, and brain fog (Fig. 2). The set of symptoms selected with and without balancing weights was the same except for shortness of breath (selected by the unadjusted model only) and abnormal movements (selected by the balancing-weighted model only). The differences in coefficients were generally small, with the largest absolute difference for brain fog (balancing weighted: 0.330 vs unadjusted: 0.497). While both models selected the same top 4 symptoms the next largest coefficients in the unadjusted model were heart palpitations and dizziness, compared to thirst and chest pain in the balancing weighted model.Fig. 2
Lasso-penalized logistic regression coefficient estimates using RECOVER-Adult data

Using the coefficients estimated under the balancing weighted analysis, we calculated an LCRI for each individual as in Eq. (3), resulting in an index ranging from 0–37 (Appendix B, Fig. B.1). Among those with history of infection, 2933 (34.1%) had an index of 0, compared to 711 (64.1%) among those without history of infection. The median and interquartile range (IQR) LCRI were 2 (0–11) and 1 (0–1), respectively.

We calculated the count of symptoms using the same set of 37 symptoms, and the correspondence between the LCRI and symptom count is illustrated in Appendix B, Fig. B.2. Among those with history of infection, 2173 (25.3%) had a count of 0 symptoms, compared to 521 (47.0%) among those without history of infection. The median (IQR) symptom counts were 3 (0–8) and 1 (0–3), respectively.

Figure 3 presents an ROC curve across all possible score thresholds, with the curve at each point on the x-axis representing the rate of LC among the uninfected for fixed rates within infected participants. Separate curves are reported for each cross-validation fold, as well as for the average over the cross-validated curves. For comparison, a curve is also reported fitting the LCRI model and estimating LC rates in the same full dataset. At any fixed rate of LC classification among the infected, the corresponding cross-validated rate of LC classification in the uninfected were lower using the LCRI than using symptom count. For example, for classification of LC among the infected at a fixed rate of 23% as reported in Thaweethai et al. [4], the estimated rate of LC classification of uninfected individuals in an independent sample is 3.8% (95% CI: 2.9–4.8.9.8%), compared to 6.4% (95% CI: 4.6–8.2.6.2%) using symptom count.Fig. 3
Receiver operator characteristic curves illustrating LC rates by infection status using LCRI and symptom count classification rules. Results shown for LC rates estimated within the same full data sample used for model fitting (dashed), and in held-out data via 10-fold cross-validation, averaging the folds (solid) and for each individual fold (light)

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In the data application, the top symptoms selected by the methodology (e.g., loss of smell/taste, PEM, brain fog, cough) coincide with those identified in descriptive reports of LC, supporting the face validity of these results [17]. At the same time, an important marker of discriminative performance for LC indices is the ability to identify LC among infected participants, with minimal misclassification of uninfected participants as having LC. While acknowledging that in the data application we can only use comparative classification behavior relative to infection history as a proxy for discriminative performance, we demonstrate that the index based on Lasso-penalized logistic regression, compared to a count of symptoms, leads to fewer misclassifications of uninfected participants as having LC while capturing the same proportion of LC cases among infected participants. Further work is needed to examine principled strategies to classify LC status based on the LCRI, based on specificity or other benchmarks [4].

Several alternative but related strategies to the one described herein are viable. For example, in the example provided, model features were defined as the presence of symptoms, while more generally, these features could capture information on incident symptoms, worsening of symptoms, or other health-related characteristics. A machine learning type approach, such as random forests [24] or support vector machines [25], could also be used in place of Lasso-penalized regression, offering the additional advantage of allowing for complex interactions and conditional associations. However, using one of these alternative supervised learning strategies does not inherently address the core challenge posed by the ‘negative-unlabeled’ data inherent in studying LC absent a gold-standard definition. Further extensions might also consider differential rates of LC by levels of a confounding variable, as well as interactions between symptoms and demographic characteristics on rates of LC.

In addition, this approach may be used to develop research indices in other settings with ‘negative-unlabeled’ data for which status is negative in one subpopulation but may be unknown in another. This may arise in other chronic sequelae of viral infection, or chronic conditions induced by exposure to stimulus, such as Lyme’s disease following tick bite.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: As the LCRI is increasingly used to characterize LC in research settings [9–11], this paper represents an important step in understanding its operating characteristics, and developing general methodology for settings with negative-unlabeled data.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: We thank the national community engagement group, caregivers, community representatives, and the participants enrolled in the RECOVER Initiative.

notes.title: Authors' contributions
notes.p: HR conceived the idea of the study. All authors designed the study. All authors participated in the development of the methods. HR performed the data analysis, interpreted the findings and drafted the manuscript. All authors revised and approved the final manuscript.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: Support for this project was provided by NIH/NHLBI R01HL162373.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: Institutional review boards at NYU Grossman School of Medicine, serving as a single institutional review board, and other participating institutions reviewed and approved the RECOVER study protocol. All participants provided written informed consent to participate in research.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
